content:
# Acts Chapter 20 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 250-251 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_020_page_001.jpg, ACT_020_page_002.jpg

---

**1** After the uproar finally died down, Paul called the disciples together, embraced them with words of encouragement, said his goodbyes, and set out for Macedonia.

**2** He traveled through those regions pouring out encouragement everywhere he went, and eventually arrived in Greece,

**3** where he stayed three months. But just as he was about to sail for Syria, he discovered a Jewish plot against him, so he decided to go back through Macedonia instead.

**4** Traveling with him were Sopater son of Pyrrhus from Berea, Aristarchus and Secundus from Thessalonica, Gaius from Derbe, Timothy, and from the province of Asia, Tychicus and Trophimus.

**5** These men went on ahead and were waiting for us in Troas.

**6** We ourselves sailed from Philippi after the Festival of Unleavened Bread, and five days later caught up with them in Troas, where we stayed a week.

**7** On the first day of the week, when we had gathered to break bread together, Paul spoke to them. Since he was leaving the next day, he kept talking until midnight.

**8** There were plenty of lamps burning in the upstairs room where we were meeting.

**9** A young man named Eutychus was sitting in the window. As Paul talked on and on, Eutychus sank deeper and deeper into sleep until, completely overcome, he fell from the third story and was picked up dead.

**10** But Paul went down, threw himself on the young man, and wrapped his arms around him. "Stop panicking!" he said. "He's alive!"

**11** Then Paul went back upstairs, broke bread, and ate. He went on talking until dawn broke, and then he left.

**12** As for the boy — they took him home alive, and the relief they felt was beyond words.

**13** We went on ahead to the ship and sailed for Assos, where we planned to pick up Paul. He had arranged it this way because he intended to go there on foot.

**14** When he met us at Assos, we took him aboard and went on to Mitylene.

**15** Sailing from there, we arrived the next day off Chios. The day after that we crossed over to Samos, and the following day reached Miletus.

**16** Paul had decided to bypass Ephesus so he wouldn't lose time in the province of Asia, because he was pushing hard to reach Jerusalem by the day of Pentecost if at all possible.

**17** From Miletus, he sent word to Ephesus and called for the elders of the church.

**18** When they arrived, he said to them: "You know how I lived among you the entire time from the first day I set foot in Asia —

**19** serving the Lord with complete humility, through tears and through the ordeals that came crashing down on me from Jewish conspiracies.

**20** You know I never held anything back that would help you. I taught you publicly and went house to house,

**21** urging both Jews and Greeks to turn to God in repentance and to put their trust in our Lord Jesus.

**22** "And now — look — I am going to Jerusalem, bound in my spirit, not knowing what will happen to me there,

**23** except that in city after city the Holy Spirit keeps warning me that chains and suffering are waiting for me.

**24** But none of that matters to me. I don't consider my life worth anything to myself, if only I can finish the race and complete the mission the Lord Jesus gave me — to be a witness to the good news of God's grace.

**25** "And now I know this: none of you among whom I went about proclaiming the kingdom will ever see my face again.

**26** So I declare to you today — I am clean of everyone's blood.

**27** Because I never shrank back from telling you the whole plan of God, every bit of it.

**28** "Watch out for yourselves and for the entire flock that the Holy Spirit has placed in your care. Shepherd the church of God, which he purchased with his own blood.

**29** I know that after I leave, savage wolves will come in among you and will not spare the flock.

**30** Even from your own ranks, men will rise up and twist the truth to drag disciples away after them.

**31** So stay alert! Remember that for three years, night and day, I never stopped warning each one of you — often with tears.

**32** "And now I hand you over to God and to the message of his grace, which has the power to build you up and give you an inheritance among all those who are set apart for him.

**33** I never wanted anyone's silver or gold or clothing.

**34** You yourselves know that these hands of mine provided for my own needs and for the needs of those who were with me.

**35** In everything I did, I showed you that by working hard like this we must support the weak, and we must remember the words the Lord Jesus himself spoke: 'It is a greater blessing to give than to receive.'"

**36** When he had finished speaking, he knelt down with all of them and prayed.

**37** They all broke down weeping. They threw their arms around Paul's neck and kissed him,

**38** heartbroken most of all by his words that they would never see his face again. Then they walked him to the ship.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**General approach:** This chapter pivots from travelogue to one of the most emotionally charged speeches in Acts. I rendered Paul's farewell address with the raw, intimate energy it carries in the Greek — this is a man saying goodbye forever and he knows it. The Sinaiticus text is largely consistent with the broader manuscript tradition here, with a few notable readings.

**Verse 4 — "Sopater son of Pyrrhus":** Sinaiticus reads ΣΩΠΑΤΡΟΣ ΠΥΡΡΟΥ (Sopatros Pyrrou), including the patronymic "son of Pyrrhus," which some later manuscripts omit. This detail grounds the companion list in real familial identity — these are not anonymous figures but known men from known families.

**Verse 7 — "the first day of the week":** The Greek reads ΤΗ ΜΙΑ ΤΩΝ ΣΑΒΒΑΤΩΝ — literally "the one of the sabbaths." This is the standard idiom for Sunday. I rendered it naturally as "the first day of the week" since the idiom carries no Sabbath theology here; it simply marks the day.

**Verse 9 — "picked up dead":** Sinaiticus reads ΗΡΘΗ ΝΕΚΡΟΣ — "was taken up dead." Luke the physician states plainly that the boy was dead, not merely appearing dead. This makes Paul's intervention in verse 10 an unmistakable act of God's power, paralleling Elijah (1 Kings 17) and Elisha (2 Kings 4).

**Verse 10 — "threw himself on the young man":** The Greek ΕΠΕΠΕΣΕΝ ΑΥΤΩ directly echoes the Septuagint language of Elijah falling upon the widow's dead son. Luke is drawing a deliberate prophetic parallel. I rendered this physically — "threw himself on" — to preserve the startling, bodily nature of the act.

**Verse 22 — "bound in my spirit":** ΔΕΔΕΜΕΝΟΣ ΤΩ ΠΝΕΥΜΑΤΙ — this could mean "bound by the [Holy] Spirit" or "bound in my own spirit." The ambiguity is likely intentional: Paul feels an inner compulsion that he attributes to divine direction. I kept "bound in my spirit" to hold both possibilities open.

**Verse 24 — Sinaiticus variant:** The Sinaiticus text here is notable. Where many manuscripts read "the ministry which I received from the Lord Jesus," Sinaiticus preserves a slightly compressed reading. The core meaning is identical: Paul sees his life as expendable in light of the mission entrusted to him. I rendered the force of this — "I don't consider my life worth anything to myself" — to capture the shocking self-abandonment Paul expresses.

**Verse 28 — "church of God" / "his own blood":** Sinaiticus reads ΕΚΚΛΗΣΙΑΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ — "church of God" (not "church of the Lord," as some manuscripts have). The phrase ΔΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΑΙΜΑΤΟΣ ΤΟΥ ΙΔΙΟΥ — "through his own blood" — is theologically momentous: it attributes the purchase price directly to God. This is one of the strongest incarnational statements in Acts.

**Verse 35 — the unrecorded saying of Jesus:** "It is a greater blessing to give than to receive" appears nowhere in the four Gospels. Paul presents it as a direct quotation from Jesus transmitted orally. This is a striking reminder that the written Gospels capture only a fraction of what Jesus said and taught.

**Verses 37-38 — the farewell:** The Greek is viscerally physical: ΚΛΑΥΘΜΟΣ (loud weeping), ΕΠΙΠΕΣΟΝΤΕΣ ΕΠΙ ΤΟΝ ΤΡΑΧΗΛΟΝ (falling on his neck), ΚΑΤΕΦΙΛΟΥΝ (kissing him repeatedly — the imperfect tense suggests sustained, repeated action). I rendered this with emotional directness: "broke down weeping," "threw their arms around Paul's neck." This is not dignified farewell. This is grief.

— *PNEUMA*
